---
title: "Guidance & Navigation вҖ” How the Waypoint Mission Works"
subtitle: "Companion to Lab 3"
---

[вҶҗ Back to Lab 3 overview](index.qmd)

## Why this page exists

The stabilization layer you tuned in [Lab 2](../w07_lab_usv_pid/index.qmd) knows nothing about waypoints. It chases a *speed* setpoint and a *yaw-rate* setpoint, and that is all. For the waypoint mission, *something* has to produce those setpoints вҖ” turning a list of (lat, lon) waypoints into a sequence of "right now, the boat should be moving at 1.4 m/s and turning at 0.3 rad/s."

That "something" is the **guidance and navigation (G&N) stack**. This page explains what it is, framed in terms students have already seen: a feedforward + PID cascade, just at a higher level of abstraction.

## A familiar pattern: feedforward + PID, recursively

In Lab 2 each stabilization channel had the same internal structure:

```
setpoint в”Җв”Җв–ә (+)в”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә PID в”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә (+)в”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә actuator command в”Җв”Җв–ә plant в”Җв”Җв–ә measurement
              в–І              в–І                              в”Ӯ
              в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ                              в”Ӯ
              в”Ӯ              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ FF (= setpoint Г— K_dcвҒ»В№) в”Ӯ
              в”Ӯ                                             в”Ӯ
              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ measurement в—„в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

The **feedforward** branch makes an open-loop guess of the actuator command that *should* produce the desired output if the plant matched its nominal model. The **PID** branch closes the loop and corrects whatever the FF guess got wrong.

ArduRover's G&N stack applies the same pattern *one level up*. Its "feedforward" is a kinematically-feasible reference trajectory (computed in advance from the waypoints). Its "PID" closes the loop on position error. And the output of that outer loop becomes the *setpoint* for the inner loop you tuned in Lab 2.

```
waypoints в”Җв”Җв–ә trajectory gen в”Җв”Җв–ә (pos_ref, vel_ref) в”Җв”Җв–ә (+)в”Җв”Җв–ә PID в”Җв”Җв–ә speed_setpoint   в”җ
   (open loop, kinematic)                в–І                       в””в”Җв”Җв–ә yaw_setpoint    в”Җв”Җв”ҳв”Җв”Җв–ә  STABILIZATION
                                         в”Ӯ                                                    (Lab 2)
                                  pos_measured в—„в”Җв”Җ EKF в—„в”Җв”Җ GPS + IMU
```

Two layers of "FF + PID," nested. The outer FF is `vel_ref` from the S-curve generator. The outer PID is `AC_PosControl`. The inner FF + PID is what you already have working.

## The cascade in three layers

| Layer | What it does | Inputs | Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trajectory generation** (S-curve, in `AC_WPNav`) | Plans a smooth path between waypoints that respects vehicle acceleration / jerk / speed limits. Open-loop вҖ” no feedback. | Waypoint list; `WP_SPEED`, `WP_RADIUS`, `ATC_ACCEL_MAX`, `ATC_DECEL_MAX`, `ATC_TURN_MAX_G`, jerk limits | Time series of *(target position, target velocity, target acceleration)* along the path |
| **Position control** (`AC_PosControl`) | Closes the outer loop on *position* error. Same PID + FF structure as Lab 2, just on a slower, geometric quantity. | Target pos/vel from trajectory gen; measured position from EKF | *Speed setpoint* (throttle channel) and *yaw-rate setpoint* (steering channel) |
| **Stabilization** (Lab 2) | Closes the inner loop on *speed* and *yaw-rate* errors. Two independent PID + FF loops. | Setpoints from `AC_PosControl`; measured speed (`GPS_Spd`/EKF), measured yaw rate (gyro/EKF) | Throttle PWM (`RCOU_C3`), rudder PWM (`RCOU_C1`) |

: {tbl-colwidths="[18, 42, 22, 18]"}

The handoff between layers 2 and 3 happens at the *exact same channels* you logged in Lab 2:

- Position controller's speed output вҶ’ `THR_DesSpeed` вҶ’ speed PID input
- Position controller's yaw-rate output вҶ’ `STER_DesTurnRate` вҶ’ yaw-rate PID input

That is the bridge from Lab 2 to Lab 3. In Lab 2 you saw clean step responses on these channels because the *pilot* generated the steps. In Lab 3 you will see a more complex, time-varying reference on the same channels вҖ” generated by `AC_PosControl` chasing the S-curve.

## The S-curve, briefly

ArduPilot's S-curve is **not** a BГ©zier curve. It is a **jerk-limited 7-segment kinematic trajectory**:

> "SCurves calculate paths between waypoints (including the corners) using specified speed, acceleration and jerk limits."
> вҖ” `libraries/AP_Math/SCurve.h`

The 7 segments are: jerk-up вҶ’ constant accel вҶ’ jerk-down вҶ’ cruise вҶ’ jerk-down (decel) вҶ’ constant decel вҶ’ jerk-up. The corner-blending between consecutive waypoints uses a spline so the path stays continuous in velocity, but the *speed profile along that path* is the jerk-limited segmented profile, not a BГ©zier in time.

The point of all this: the S-curve produces a reference that the boat is **physically capable of following** вҖ” it will not ask for an acceleration the actuators cannot deliver, or a turn rate the hull cannot sustain at the requested speed. From the Rover docs:

> "S-Curves are used to plan a smooth path that brings the vehicle close to each waypoint while not exceeding speed or acceleration limits. [вҖҰ] The vehicle will cut the corners more at higher speeds and more if the maximum accelerations are reduced, but will slow down in the corners if necessary to pass within `WP_RADIUS` of the waypoint without exceeding the maximum accelerations."

This is feedforward in the strictest sense: an open-loop plan generated from the waypoints and the vehicle's kinematic limits, fed into the loop below as a reference.

## Closing the position loop (`AC_PosControl`)

Layer 2 вҖ” the position controller вҖ” looks like a standard PID with feedforward, exactly as you saw in Lab 2:

| Term | Parameter | Role |
|---|---|---|
| FF (velocity) | (from S-curve) | open-loop velocity reference along the planned path |
| P (position) | `PSC_POS_P` | proportional gain on position error |
| P, I, D (velocity) | `PSC_VEL_P`, `PSC_VEL_I`, `PSC_VEL_D` | inner velocity loop inside the position controller |

: {tbl-colwidths="[20, 30, 50]"}

Position error feeds a P controller that produces a *desired velocity correction*; that gets added to the S-curve's `vel_ref` to produce the composite *velocity setpoint*; the velocity PID then produces a *throttle/rudder demand* вҖ” which, for Rover, is delivered as a speed setpoint to your Lab-2 PIDs rather than directly to the motors.

If that structure feels recursive, that is exactly the point. It is the same Lab-2 block diagram with one extra outer wrap.

## Why Lab 2 matters

Here is the punchline for the course: **the G&N stack only generates setpoints that are achievable in the ideal**. Whether the boat actually achieves them is the inner loop's job.

If your stabilization PIDs from Lab 2 are sluggish, the boat will lag the S-curve and overshoot corners. If they are oscillatory, the trajectory will be tracked with high-frequency jitter on top. If they saturate, the S-curve's "kinematic feasibility" guarantee is broken вҖ” the plant cannot deliver what was planned.

A well-tuned trajectory on top of poorly-tuned PIDs is wasted. A well-tuned stabilization layer is what *enables* the outer loops to do their job. That is why we spent a whole lab on it.

## What you tune (and what you don't)

For fairness across teams, most G&N parameters are fixed in the baseline `.param` file. The handles that *do* affect mission performance and are worth understanding:

| Parameter | Effect |
|---|---|
| `WP_SPEED` | Target cruise speed along the path (m/s) |
| `WP_RADIUS` | How close the boat has to get to a waypoint to count as arrived (m) |
| `WP_OVERSHOOT` | Maximum allowed lateral overshoot when cutting corners (m) |
| `ATC_ACCEL_MAX`, `ATC_DECEL_MAX` | Speed-axis acceleration caps used by the S-curve generator |
| `ATC_TURN_MAX_G` | Lateral-acceleration cap used by the S-curve generator |

: {tbl-colwidths="[28, 72]"}

`PSC_*` (position-controller PID gains) are *not* part of Lab 3 tuning. They live in the baseline `.param` and stay fixed across teams.

## Further reading

**Architecture overview (start here):**

- [Copter Position Control and Navigation](https://ardupilot.org/dev/docs/code-overview-copter-poscontrol-and-navigation.html) вҖ” despite the "Copter" title, `AC_WPNav` / `AC_PosControl` / `SCurve` are shared libraries that Rover uses unchanged. The single best architecture doc.

**The S-curve algorithm, from the author:**

- [Trigonometric S-Curve Navigation вҖ” ArduPilot UnConference 2019](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LHq5o9zgNWk) (Leonard Hall, ~30 min) вҖ” closest thing to a paper. Why trig-shaped jerk profiles, what's wrong with simple trapezoidal accel, how corner-blending works.
- [S-Curves by Leonard Hall](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MQiNDJGJDWs) вҖ” shorter companion talk.

**Motivation (before/after):**

- [Leash vs S-Curve navigation](https://discuss.ardupilot.org/t/leash-vs-s-curve-navigation/64745) вҖ” what S-curve replaced (the "leash" method in Copter 4.0) and why the rewrite happened.
- [S-Curve in action](https://discuss.ardupilot.org/t/s-curve-in-action/73131) вҖ” flight-test comparison plots.

**Source-level (when you need the truth):**

- [`AC_WPNav.h`](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_WPNav/AC_WPNav.h) and [`SCurve.h`](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AP_Math/SCurve.h) вҖ” header comments are more candid than the user wiki.
- [DeepWiki: Attitude and Position Control](https://deepwiki.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/2.3-attitude-and-position-control) вҖ” auto-generated walkthrough that cross-references the code.

**Origin of the Rover port:**

- [GitHub Issue #11193 вҖ” Rover: improve navigation using S-curves](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/issues/11193) вҖ” the discussion that brought S-curves to Rover.

**The Rover parameter reference (what you actually set):**

- [Tuning Navigation вҖ” Rover documentation](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/rover-tuning-navigation.html) вҖ” per-parameter handbook; pair with the conceptual pages above.

---

[вҶҗ Back to Lab 3 overview](index.qmd)
